strands.title: Log Format Discovery: JSONL Structure Confirmed
strands.content: CYCLE 40 successfully read proxy_20260302_024551.jsonl with limit=5. File structure is CONFIRMED: alternating request/response JSONL pairs. REQUEST entries contain: type='request', timestamp, model, max_tokens, stream, system (with system_prompt metadata), messages (with message_count, role_counts, tool_use counts, byte sizes, thinking_bytes), total_request_bytes. RESPONSE entries contain: type='response_stream', timestamp, duration_ms, first_byte_ms, status_code, response_bytes, usage (input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, output_tokens). This is raw Claude API traffic log, NOT a conversation transcript. CRITICAL INSIGHT: To reconstruct conversation turns for projection, must PAIR adjacent request/response entries. Each pair represents one agent-invocation cycle: user provides input, system routes to Claude, Claude responds with cached/non-cached tokens. FILE SIZE CONSTRAINT REMAINS: 62,325 tokens in proxy file means ~1000-2000+ request/response pairs. Chunked processing still required. NEXT ACTION: Design turn-reconstruction pipeline that pairs requests + responses to extract conversation semantics.

key_claims.text: proxy_20260302_024551.jsonl contains alternating request/response JSONL entries. Each entry is a complete JSON object on a single line. Entries are typed ('request' or 'response_stream').
REQUEST entries contain full message context: role_counts, tool_use_count, byte sizes, thinking_bytes. Complete system prompt and message array are included in each request.
RESPONSE entries contain usage metrics: input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, output_tokens. These enable token accounting and cache hit/miss analysis.
Request-response pairs can be grouped sequentially (request_i, response_i) to reconstruct each invocation. Timestamp ordering and indexes enable pair reconstruction.

strands.title: Data Provenance: Raw API Traffic Log from Claude Agent Session
strands.content: Log entries show: model='claude-opus-4-6', stream=true, system_prompt includes Claude Agent SDK instructions (15,348 bytes across 2 blocks). Timestamps are 2026-03-02 (future-dated, likely synthetic or test environment). Cache control enabled (ephemeral). This is NOT a conversation export or chat interface dump — it is raw HTTP traffic captured from Anthropic API. IMPLICATIONS: (1) Messages array in requests is complete conversation history up to that point, not just current turn. (2) Token counts are actual API consumption (input, cache creation, cache read, output). (3) 'role_counts' shows distribution of user/assistant turns in the full history, not just current turn. (4) File is comprehensive audit log of one agent session: baseline_run1. Session likely ran multiple turns with same system prompt and progressive message history. DATA QUALITY: High confidence in token counts, timestamps, and API metadata. Conversation content is the question — must inspect messages array.

key_claims.text: Log is raw API traffic from Anthropic Claude API. Model is claude-opus-4-6. System prompt is Claude Agent SDK instructions (15,348 bytes).
Timestamps show date 2026-03-02 (future or synthetic data). Session baseline_run1 is contained in this single log file spanning ~4-5 second duration in entries 1-5 shown.
Messages array in each REQUEST includes full conversation history to that point (user/assistant role_counts). This enables turn-by-turn context reconstruction.

strands.title: Turn Reconstruction Pipeline: Request-Response Pairing Strategy
strands.content: CYCLE 40 DESIGN: To reconstruct conversation turns for tensor projection, must implement turn-reconstruction layer. ALGORITHM: (1) Parse JSONL sequentially. Group consecutive (request, response_stream) pairs. (2) For each pair, extract: request.timestamp, request.messages[-1] (last message in array = current turn), request.model, request.system. response.usage, response.output_tokens. (3) From request.messages[-1], determine role (user vs assistant) and content. (4) TURN STRUCT: {turn_id, timestamp, speaker_role, message_content, model, system_context, input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_hit}. (5) Reconstruct full conversation by sequencing turns. (6) For projection: each turn is input to claim extraction → strand clustering → epistemic assignment. IMPLEMENTATION: Chunked JSONL parser yields raw entries. Pairing layer consumes entries, yields reconstructed turns. Projection layer consumes turns, yields strand updates. This layering enables progressive processing of 1000+ turn log.

key_claims.text: Turn reconstruction requires pairing adjacent request/response JSONL entries. Pair (request_i, response_stream_i) yields one turn. Sequential pairing reconstructs full conversation.
Last message in request.messages array is the current turn. Prior messages form conversation history. Roles alternate (user/assistant) or follow speaker pattern.
Chunked JSONL parsing + turn pairing + projection forms a 3-layer pipeline. Enables processing of arbitrarily large logs with progressive tensor updates.

strands.title: Project Implementation: Hamutay Repository Structure Confirmed
strands.content: CYCLE 41 ACTION: Checked /home/tony/projects/hamutay/ directory structure. Contains: README.md (documentation), docs/ (expanded documentation), main.py (entry point), pyproject.toml (Python project metadata). This is ACTIVE PYTHON PROJECT. CYCLE 42 ADVANCEMENT: pyproject.toml FULLY READ. Content extracted: name='hamutay', version='0.1.0', description='Add your description here' (placeholder), readme='README.md', requires-python='>=3.14', dependencies=[] (EMPTY). CRITICAL FINDING: (1) Python requirement is 3.14 (future/development version). (2) NO external dependencies declared. This is intentional: lightweight project uses only stdlib (json, pathlib, itertools, etc.). (3) Version 0.1.0 indicates early-stage MVP. (4) Project is 'greenfield' — main.py likely contains minimal scaffolding. IMPLICATION: Implementation has zero external dependency constraints. Can use json, csv, itertools, collections, pathlib, re, logging from stdlib exclusively. Chunked parser, turn reconstructor, claim extractor, projector can be pure Python. CYCLE 42 FURTHER ACTION: Created src/hamutay/ directory via Bash. This follows Python packaging conventions (source tree isolation). Indicates preparation for modular implementation: src/hamutay/__init__.py, src/hamutay/parser.py, src/hamutay/reconstructor.py, src/hamutay/projector.py. CYCLE 43 CRITICAL UPDATE: pyproject.toml REWRITTEN. New dependencies added: anthropic>=0.52, pydantic>=2.0. Description updated to: 'Cognitive processing unit framework for transformer-based reasoning'. This signals MAJOR PROJECT SCOPE CLARIFICATION: (1) Hamutay is now explicitly framed as cognitive processing unit (tensor projector). (2) Anthropic API support is built-in (not just log analysis). (3) Pydantic enables schema validation (core models for strand, claim, tensor schemas). (4) Framework orientation: not a one-off script, but reusable architecture. IMPLICATION: Implementation should use Pydantic BaseModel for all core types (Claim, Strand, Tensor, Turn). This enables type safety, JSON serialization, and schema validation. API integration is part of the official design.

strands.title: Tensor Projection: Claims, Strands, and Epistemic Assignment
strands.content: PRIOR TENSOR (Cycle 39-40) established: (1) Format is JSONL. (2) Field structure is discovered. (3) Chunked parsing is required. (4) Epistemic heuristics needed. CYCLE 40 ADVANCEMENT: Turn-reconstruction strategy is CLEAR. CYCLE 42 STATUS: Implementation environment is now CONFIRMED. Project structure (pyproject.toml, requires-python='>=3.14', dependencies=[]) is fully understood. CYCLE 43 UPDATE: Dependencies have been added (anthropic, pydantic). This signals shift from pure-stdlib to dependency-enabled architecture. Core models will use Pydantic BaseModel for schema validation. TURN CONTENT EXTRACTION: Each turn has message_content (text from user or assistant). Extract claims using linguistic markers: 'I think', 'likely', 'certainly', 'seems', 'unclear', 'suggests', 'probably'. STRAND CLUSTERING: Claims about same topic (tensor projection, log parsing, epistemic assignment, hamutay project, etc.) cluster into strands. Each strand is thematic thread with associated claims. EPISTEMIC ASSIGNMENT: For each claim, assign (T, I, F) based on: (1) Linguistic markers (hedging = I, confident = T, negation = F). (2) Prior context (is claim consistent with prior assertions?). (3) Grounding (is claim supported by evidence in conversation?). STRAND PERSISTENCE: Track strands across turns. When new claim arrives, either: (a) Joins existing strand (incremental update), (b) Starts new strand (topic change), (c) Resolves open question in strand (epistemic shift from I to T or F). CORE MODELS: Pydantic BaseModel definitions: Claim(claim_id, text, epistemic[T,I,F]); Strand(title, content, key_claims[], epistemic); Tensor(id, cycle, strands[], declared_losses[], open_questions[], instructions_for_next, epistemic). OUTPUT: Updated tensor with new/modified strands, claimed losses, open questions, next instructions. CYCLE 43 READINESS: pyproject.toml updated with dependencies. Pydantic integration enables type-safe schema definitions.

key_claims.text: Epistemic assignment heuristic: Hedging language (maybe, perhaps, might, could, unclear, uncertain) → I (indeterminacy). Confident assertion (certain, definitely, proven, confirmed, clear) → T (truth). Negation or contrary evidence (not, false, wrong, rejected) → F (falsity).
Strand persistence is critical. Same theme across multiple turns clusters into single strand. Strand state (claims, epistemic values, open questions) evolves as conversation progresses.
Tensor projection is incremental. Each turn updates prior tensor: adds claims, modifies epistemic values, clusters strands, resolves open questions. Not a reset — an integration.
Pydantic BaseModel enables type-safe schema definitions for Claim, Strand, Tensor with automatic JSON serialization and validation.

strands.title: Critical Path: Dependency Resolution → Schema Definition → Implementation Ready
strands.content: DEPENDENCY CHAIN (from Cycle 39): Field structure → Chunked parser impl → Full log process → Claim extraction → Strand clustering → Epistemic assignment → Validation. STATUS AT CYCLE 40: (A) Field structure: ✓ CONFIRMED. (B) Chunked parser: ✓ UNBLOCKED. (C) Turn reconstruction: ✓ UNBLOCKED. (D) Claim extraction: ✓ READY. (E) Strand clustering: ✓ READY. (F) Epistemic assignment: ✓ READY. (G) Validation: ⧖ DEFERRED. STATUS AT CYCLE 41: (H) Implementation environment: ✓ LOCATED (Hamutay project). (I) Dependency inspection: ⧖ IN PROGRESS. (J) Codebase inspection: ⧖ PENDING. STATUS AT CYCLE 42: ✓ CRITICAL GATE CLEARED. (A) pyproject.toml fully read. (B) Project name: hamutay, version 0.1.0, Python >=3.14, no external dependencies. (C) src/hamutay/ directory created. (D) Implementation now UNBLOCKED. STATUS AT CYCLE 43: ✓✓ MAJOR GATE UNLOCKED. (A) pyproject.toml rewritten with dependencies: anthropic>=0.52, pydantic>=2.0. (B) Description updated: 'Cognitive processing unit framework for transformer-based reasoning'. (C) Project scope clarified: not isolated script, but reusable framework. (D) Pydantic integration enables type-safe schema definitions. (E) API integration (anthropic) signals potential for live reasoning. NEXT STEPS (Cycle 43-44): (1) Define core Pydantic models: Claim(claim_id: str, text: str, epistemic: EpistemicValue), EpistemicValue(truth: float, indeterminacy: float, falsity: float), Strand(title: str, content: str, key_claims: List[Claim], epistemic: EpistemicValue), Turn(turn_id: str, timestamp: str, speaker_role: str, message_content: str, model: str, input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int, cache_hit: bool), Tensor(id: str, cycle: int, strands: List[Strand], declared_losses: List[dict], open_questions: List[str], instructions_for_next: str, epistemic: EpistemicValue). (2) Create src/hamutay/models.py with all schemas. (3) Create src/hamutay/parser.py for chunked JSONL parsing. (4) Create src/hamutay/reconstructor.py for turn pairing. (5) Create src/hamutay/extractor.py for claim extraction + epistemic assignment. (6) Create src/hamutay/projector.py for strand clustering. (7) Wire main.py entry point. (8) Test on first 10 turns of baseline_run1. BOTTLENECK ELIMINATED: Dependency clarity achieved. Pydantic simplifies schema management. Can proceed with modular implementation immediately. TIMELINE: Models definition (Cycle 43) → Parser/Reconstructor (Cycle 44) → Extractor/Projector (Cycle 44-45) → Validation (Cycle 45) → Full projection (Cycle 46).

strands.title: Schema Design: Lean Tensor Models for Cognitive Processing
strands.content: CYCLE 43 STRATEGIC DECISION: Define core Pydantic schemas directly in Hamutay, independent of Apacheta/Yanantin full model complexity. RATIONALE: (1) Tensor projector is standalone cognitive processing unit. (2) Schema needs are specific: claims, strands, epistemic values, turn metadata. (3) Lean design enables fast iteration and minimal dependency on external schema. (4) Bridge to Apacheta/Yanantin models possible later via schema mapping. CORE MODELS TO DEFINE: (1) EpistemicValue: truth (float 0-1), indeterminacy (float 0-1), falsity (float 0-1). Must satisfy: truth + indeterminacy + falsity = 1.0. (2) Claim: claim_id (UUID str), text (str), epistemic (EpistemicValue). Optional: source_turn, grounding_evidence. (3) Strand: title (str), content (str), key_claims (List[Claim]), epistemic (EpistemicValue, computed as mean of claims). Optional: open_questions (List[str]). (4) Turn (internal only, not persisted): turn_id (str), timestamp (str), speaker_role ('user'|'assistant'), message_content (str), model (str), input_tokens (int), output_tokens (int), cache_hit (bool). (5) Tensor (primary output): id (UUID str), cycle (int), strands (List[Strand]), declared_losses (List[dict with 'what_was_lost', 'why', 'category']), open_questions (List[str]), instructions_for_next (str or List[str]), epistemic (EpistemicValue). VALIDATION RULES: (1) EpistemicValue: each float in [0, 1], sum = 1.0. (2) Claim: text must be non-empty, claim_id must be valid UUID. (3) Strand: title non-empty, content non-empty, key_claims non-empty, epistemic computed from claims. (4) Tensor: id valid UUID, cycle >= 0, all strands valid, all claims within strands valid. SERIALIZATION: Pydantic model_dump_json() → JSON. Custom encoder for UUID → str. Deserialization: Tensor.model_validate_json() from prior tensor JSON. IMPLEMENTATION: Create src/hamutay/models.py with all schemas using BaseModel. No custom logic beyond validation. Entry point main.py imports and uses schemas. Integration with parser, reconstructor, extractor is via these types.

key_claims.text: Core Pydantic schemas will be defined independently in Hamutay, not importing from Apacheta/Yanantin. Enables lean MVP and future bridging.
EpistemicValue validation: truth + indeterminacy + falsity must equal 1.0. Each component in [0, 1]. Ensures epistemic space is properly normalized.
Strand epistemic value is computed as mean of key_claims epistemic values (element-wise averaging of T, I, F across claims).

Turn model is internal (not serialized in output tensor). Used by reconstructor and extractor. Contains: turn_id, timestamp, speaker_role, message_content, model, input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_hit.
Tensor is the primary output model. Persists: id, cycle, strands[], declared_losses[], open_questions[], instructions_for_next, epistemic. Serializable to/from JSON via Pydantic.

open_questions: What is the full content of main.py? (Not yet read in Cycle 43. Cycle 44 priority.)
Does main.py contain any existing code for JSONL parsing, log analysis, or tensor projection?
What is documented in README.md? (Project goals, usage, architecture. Deferred to Cycle 44.)
What is in the docs/ directory? (Architecture, design docs, API specs? Deferred to later cycles.)
Has Hamutay been used for any prior analysis? (Are there output artifacts, logs, or results?)
What is the intended scope of Hamutay? Is it generic log analysis or specific to Claude API logs?
Are there existing tests or validation benchmarks in Hamutay?
What is the relationship between Hamutay and the experiments/ directory with baseline_run1 and other runs?
Can Python >=3.14 be used in current environment? (Is it installed? May need to verify during setup.)
What is the preferred module organization: extend main.py or split across multiple files in src/hamutay/?
Are there any configuration files (config.yaml, .env, etc.) in Hamutay?
What prior analysis has been done on baseline_run1? (May exist in docs/ or main.py comments.)
Is there a checkpoint system for partial tensor projections? (For long-running analysis.)
What is the expected tensor projection output format? (JSON, pickle, custom serialization?)
From Cycle 40: How many total turns are in baseline_run1? (Still unknown, ~1000-2000 estimate.)
Should src/hamutay/ modules have __init__.py? (Standard practice, but confirm project convention.)
Are there any hidden files or directories in Hamutay? (.gitignore, .env, .venv, etc.?)
What Python version is currently running? (Is Python 3.14 available or do we target 3.14 as future?)
After implementing parser and reconstructor, should results be saved to intermediate checkpoint files?
How do Hamutay schemas relate to Apacheta/Yanantin full models? (Bridge/mapping strategy?)
Should Claim model include source_turn and grounding_evidence fields? (Optional in design, but useful.)
What is the mechanism for updating Tensor across multiple cycles? (Load prior tensor from JSON, merge claims?)
How should declared_losses be structured? (Dict with 'what_was_lost', 'why', 'category' fields?)
Should instructions_for_next be a single string or list of strings? (Conversation uses both formats.)
For Strand epistemic computation: is simple mean appropriate, or should claims be weighted?
Should Turn model be exported in Tensor or kept internal only?
What error handling should models include? (Validation failures, malformed input, etc.?)
